title: Bike Lanes vs Business Access
description: A corridor redesign would replace curb parking with protected bike lanes.

participants.name: Iris
participants.statement: Protected bike lanes will make the street safer and reduce car dependency.

participants.name: Dev
participants.statement: Removing curb parking will hurt shops because customers need quick access.

participants.name: Cam
participants.statement: Traffic violence is a safety issue, and bike lanes are a proven solution.

participants.name: Lena
participants.statement: I support safer streets, but accessible drop-off zones must remain nearby.

participants.name: Ravi
participants.statement: The city should publish data about loading demand before construction.

participants.name: Tess
participants.statement: Business owners feel ignored when street changes are announced late.

participants.name: Hana
participants.statement: I'm in a wheelchair. Curb cuts and ADA-compliant loading zones can't be afterthoughts when the street is rebuilt.

participants.name: Eduardo
participants.statement: I run a family restaurant on this corridor. Our delivery drivers double-park as it is. Take more curb away and we'll lose dinner-rush orders.

participants.name: Mei
participants.statement: I'm 71 and I walk this street every morning. Cars speed past me. A protected lane buffer would actually make me feel safer crossing.

participants.name: Jamal
participants.statement: I bike to work daily. Painted lanes don't protect anyone. We need real concrete separation, not another half-measure.

participants.name: Sofia
participants.statement: I drive the local bus on this route. Stops need clear sightlines for boarding. Any bike-lane plan has to coordinate with transit, not fight it.

participants.name: Beatrice
participants.statement: I'm a single mom with three kids. I need to park close to drop-off and then run errands. If parking disappears, my whole morning falls apart.
